title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers and a target, return the indices of two numbers that add up to the target. Each input has exactly one solution and you may not use the same element twice.
hints: You need O(1) lookup for complements.
As you scan, store what you've seen so far.

approach: Create a hash map from value → index.
For each number, check if (target - number) exists in the map.
If found, return both indices. Otherwise, add the current number to the map.
solution.keyInsight: Instead of checking every pair (O(n²)), store seen values in a hash map so complement lookup is O(1).

title: Valid Palindrome
description: Given a string, determine if it reads the same forward and backward after considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring case.
hints: Compare characters from both ends.
Skip non-alphanumeric characters as you go.

approach: Place left pointer at start and right pointer at end.
Skip non-alphanumeric chars on either side.
Compare lowercase versions; move inward on match, return false on mismatch.
Continue until pointers cross.
solution.keyInsight: Two pointers converging from both ends let you verify symmetry in a single pass without extra space.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string, find the length of the longest substring that contains no repeating characters.
hints: Expand a window and track characters inside it.
When you see a duplicate, shrink from the left.

approach: Use a hash set (or map of last index) to track characters in the current window.
Expand right pointer; if char is duplicate, shrink left until it's unique again.
Track the maximum window size seen.
solution.keyInsight: A sliding window maintains the valid substring invariant — expand to grow, shrink to fix violations.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: A sorted array has been rotated at an unknown pivot. Given a target, return its index or -1 if not found. Assume no duplicates.
hints: One half of the array is always sorted.
Decide which half to search based on where the target could lie.

approach: Binary search with left and right pointers.
Identify which half is sorted (compare mid with left).
Check if target falls in the sorted half; adjust search range accordingly.
Repeat until found or range is empty.
solution.keyInsight: Even after rotation, binary search works because at least one half remains sorted, letting you eliminate half each step.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given a 2D grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), count the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.
hints: Each unvisited '1' starts a new island.
You need to mark all connected land cells as visited.

approach: Iterate through every cell in the grid.
When you find a '1', increment island count and run DFS/BFS to sink the entire island (mark visited).
DFS explores all 4 adjacent directions recursively.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(m × n) worst case for recursion stack
solution.keyInsight: DFS (or BFS) flood-fills each connected component. The number of flood-fills equals the number of islands.

title: Rotting Oranges
description: In a grid, each cell is empty, a fresh orange, or a rotten orange. Every minute, rotten oranges rot adjacent fresh ones (4-directionally). Return the minimum minutes until no fresh orange remains, or -1 if impossible.
hints: Rotting spreads simultaneously from all rotten oranges.
Track time level by level.

approach: Enqueue all initially rotten oranges with time 0.
BFS: for each rotten orange, rot adjacent fresh ones and enqueue them with time+1.
Track fresh count; decrement when rotting.
Return max time if fresh count is 0, else -1.
solution.keyInsight: BFS naturally processes simultaneous spread level by level, giving the minimum time for multi-source propagation.

title: Climbing Stairs
description: You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Given n steps, how many distinct ways can you reach the top?
hints: Ways to reach step n = ways to reach (n-1) + ways to reach (n-2).
This looks like Fibonacci.

approach: Define dp[i] = number of ways to reach step i.
Recurrence: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].
Optimize to O(1) space by tracking only the last two values.
solution.keyInsight: Optimal substructure: every way to reach step n ends with either a 1-step or 2-step, so sum the two subproblems.

title: Coin Change
description: Given coin denominations and an amount, return the fewest number of coins needed to make that amount. Return -1 if impossible.
hints: For each amount, try every coin and take the minimum.
Build up from smaller amounts to the target.

approach: dp[a] = min coins to make amount a. Initialize dp[0]=0, rest=∞.
For each amount from 1 to target, try each coin: dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1).
Return dp[amount] if finite, else -1.
solution.keyInsight: Unbounded knapsack style DP: the optimal solution for amount a uses one coin plus the optimal solution for a - coin.

title: Jump Game
description: Given an array where nums[i] is the max jump length from position i, determine if you can reach the last index starting from index 0.
hints: Track the farthest index you can reach.
If current index exceeds farthest reachable, you're stuck.

approach: Maintain farthest reachable index, initially 0.
At each position i, if i > farthest, return false.
Update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]).
If farthest >= last index, return true.
solution.keyInsight: Greedy works: you only care about the maximum reachable position, not the specific path taken.

title: Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string containing only "(", ")", "{", "}", "[", and "]", determine if the input string is valid. Open brackets must close in the correct order.
hints: Most recent unmatched open bracket must match the next close.
LIFO order suggests a stack.

approach: Push opening brackets onto a stack.
On closing bracket, check if stack top is the matching opener; pop if so, else invalid.
After scanning, stack must be empty.
solution.keyInsight: Nested brackets require LIFO matching — the exact behavior of a stack.

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
description: Given an integer array and k, return the kth largest element. Note that it is the kth largest in sorted order, not the kth distinct.
hints: You don't need full sort — just track the k largest.
A min-heap of size k keeps the kth largest at the top.

approach: Maintain a min-heap of size k.
For each element, add to heap; if size > k, pop the smallest.
After processing all elements, heap top is the kth largest.
solution.keyInsight: A size-k min-heap acts as a sliding window of the k largest elements seen so far.

title: Number of Provinces
description: There are n cities. A matrix shows direct connections between cities. Cities are in the same province if directly or indirectly connected. Return the total number of provinces.
hints: Transitive connections form groups.
Merge connected cities and count distinct groups.

approach: Initialize Union-Find with n components.
For each connected pair (i, j) in the matrix, union(i, j).
Return the number of distinct roots (provinces).
solution.keyInsight: Union-Find efficiently merges connected components and counts remaining groups after processing all edges.

title: Course Schedule
description: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to n-1. Prerequisites are given as pairs [a, b] meaning you must take b before a. Return true if you can finish all courses.
hints: This is about detecting cycles in a dependency graph.
Process courses with no remaining prerequisites first.

approach: Build adjacency list and in-degree count for each course.
Enqueue all courses with in-degree 0.
BFS (Kahn's algorithm): dequeue a course, reduce in-degree of neighbors, enqueue new zeros.
If processed count equals numCourses, no cycle exists.
solution.keyInsight: Topological sort detects cycles in a DAG — if you can't process all nodes, a circular dependency exists.

title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
description: Design a data structure that supports inserting words, searching for exact words, and checking if any word starts with a given prefix.
hints: Characters form a tree path from root to leaf.
Each node represents a character prefix.

approach: Each TrieNode has children map and an isEnd flag.
Insert: walk/create nodes for each character, mark isEnd at the last char.
Search: follow children; return isEnd for exact match.
StartsWith: follow children; return true if path exists.
solution.timeComplexity: O(m) per operation, m = word length
solution.keyInsight: A trie shares common prefixes across words, making prefix queries natural and efficient.

title: Subsets
description: Given an array of unique integers, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets.
hints: For each element, choose to include it or not.
Build candidates incrementally and backtrack.

approach: Sort the array (optional, for consistency).
Use backtracking: at each index, choose to include or exclude the element.
When index reaches end, add current subset to results.
Backtrack by removing the last choice.
solution.keyInsight: Backtracking explores the binary decision tree of include/exclude for each element, generating all 2^n subsets.

title: Daily Temperatures
description: Given an array of daily temperatures, return an array where answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after day i for a warmer temperature. If no future day is warmer, answer[i] = 0.
hints: For each day, you need the next greater element to the right.
A decreasing stack of indices tracks unresolved days.

approach: Iterate with a monotonic decreasing stack of indices.
While current temp > temp at stack top, pop and set answer[popped] = i - popped.
Push current index onto stack.
solution.keyInsight: A monotonic stack efficiently finds the next greater element by discarding indices that can't be answers for future days.

title: Subarray Sum Equals K
description: Given an array of integers and an integer k, return the total number of continuous subarrays whose sum equals k.
hints: If prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k, subarray (i, j] sums to k.
Track prefix sums and how often each has been seen.

approach: Maintain a running prefix sum and a map of prefix sum → count.
For each prefix sum, add map[prefix - k] to the answer (those are valid starting points).
Increment the count for the current prefix sum.
solution.keyInsight: Prefix sums convert the subarray sum problem into a complement lookup: if prefix[j] - k existed before, that's a valid subarray ending at j.

title: Container With Most Water
description: Given n non-negative integers representing vertical lines, find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water.
hints: Start with the widest container (outer pointers).
Move the pointer at the shorter line inward — that's the only way to potentially improve.

approach: Set left = 0, right = n-1.
Compute area = min(height[l], height[r]) × (r - l), track max.
Move the pointer at the shorter height inward.
Repeat until pointers meet.
solution.keyInsight: Greedy two-pointer: shrinking from the shorter side is safe because keeping it can't increase the limiting height while reducing width.

title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [start, end], merge all overlapping intervals and return the result.
hints: Sort intervals by start time.
Compare each interval with the last merged one.

approach: Sort intervals by start time.
Initialize result with the first interval.
For each next interval: if it overlaps the last merged (start ≤ last end), extend last end; else append.
solution.keyInsight: After sorting by start, overlapping intervals are adjacent — a single pass merge suffices.

title: Max Consecutive Ones III
description: Given a binary array and an integer k, return the maximum number of consecutive 1's in the array if you can flip at most k 0's to 1's.
hints: Find the longest window with at most k zeros.
Shrink when zeros in window exceed k.

approach: Sliding window with left/right pointers and a zero count.
Expand right; when zeros > k, shrink left until zeros ≤ k.
Track max window size.
solution.keyInsight: The problem becomes: find the longest subarray containing at most k zeros — a classic variable sliding window.
